title: The Dative Case
summary: The dative case marks the indirect object of a verb — the person or thing to whom or for whom something is done. All three genders change their article forms, and plural nouns gain an extra -n in the dative.

sections.heading: Articles in the dative case
body: In the dative case, all genders change their article forms. Masculine and neuter use dem (definite) / einem (indefinite). Feminine uses der (definite) / einer (indefinite). The plural definite article is den, and all plural nouns add -n to their plural form if they do not already end in -n or -s.
To identify the indirect object, ask 'Wem?' (to whom?). For example: 'Ich gebe dem Kind ein Buch.' → 'Wem gebe ich das Buch? Dem Kind.' — dem signals that Kind (neuter) is the indirect object.

examples.de: Sie gibt der Frau ein Geschenk.
examples.en: She gives the woman a present. (fem.)

sections.heading: Dative prepositions
body: Certain prepositions always require the dative case. The most important ones are: aus (out of / from), bei (at / with / near), mit (with), nach (after / to), seit (since / for), von (from / by / of), zu (to), gegenüber (opposite / across from). A helpful mnemonic for the first seven is 'aus bei mit nach seit von zu'.

After any of these prepositions, you must use the dative article forms. Note the common contractions: bei + dem = beim, von + dem = vom, zu + dem = zum, zu + der = zur. For example: 'Ich gehe zum Supermarkt.' (zu + dem Supermarkt → zum).

examples.de: Nach dem Unterricht gehe ich nach Hause.
Ich lerne seit einem Jahr Deutsch.
examples.en: I have been learning German for a year.

examples.de: Das ist ein Geschenk von meiner Freundin.
examples.en: That is a present from my (girl)friend.

examples.de: Die Bäckerei ist gegenüber dem Bahnhof.
examples.en: The bakery is opposite the station.

sections.heading: Dative verbs and indirect objects
body: Some German verbs always take a dative object. The most common are: helfen (to help), danken (to thank), gehören (to belong to), gefallen (to please / to like), glauben (to believe), folgen (to follow), antworten (to answer). These verbs describe an action directed at a person, but grammatically that person is dative, not accusative.

When a sentence has both a direct object (accusative) and an indirect object (dative), the usual word order is: dative first, then accusative — unless the accusative is a pronoun, in which case the pronoun comes first. Example: 'Ich zeige dem Kind das Buch.' (dative noun before accusative noun) vs. 'Ich zeige ihm das Buch.' (dative pronoun is still first here).

examples.en: The book belongs to my brother.
I like the music a lot. (lit.: The music pleases me a lot.)

examples.de: Ich schicke meiner Mutter eine Nachricht.
examples.en: I am sending my mother a message.

exercises.prompt: Ich fahre mit ___ Zug. (der Zug – masculine)
exercises.explanation: mit always takes the dative. The dative definite article for masculine nouns is dem.

exercises.question: Which sentence is grammatically correct?
exercises.explanation: bei takes the dative. The plural dative is den, and plural nouns add -n: die Freunde → den Freunden.

exercises.prompt: Das Buch gehört ___ Lehrerin. (die Lehrerin – feminine)
exercises.explanation: gehören takes a dative object. The dative definite article for feminine nouns is der.

exercises.question: "I have been living here since a year." Choose the correct German phrase:
exercises.explanation: seit takes the dative. Jahr is neuter; the indefinite dative article for neuter is einem: seit einem Jahr.
The verb (gibt) takes position 2. The dative indirect object (ihrer Schwester) comes before the accusative direct object (das Buch).
